title: Excel Sheets, Existential Crises, and Occasional Glory
body: Excel Sheets, Existential Crises, and Occasional Glory
pros: The risk models are complex enough to make you question reality â but in a good way. Colleagues are brilliant, slightly caffeinated, and fluent in sarcasm. Youâll learn more acronyms than a military strategist and use them in casual conversation. The office lighting is optimized for both productivity and dramatic monologues. Free fruit on Wednesdays. Sometimes itâs exotic. Once we had a durian. It was a moment.
cons: The coffee machine has a vendetta against Risk. It only works for Compliance. Meetings occasionally spiral into philosophical debates about the nature of âacceptable variance.â Youâll dream in spreadsheets. Conditional formatting will haunt you. The printer on Floor 3 is sentient and judges your formatting choices.

title: Emotional Rollercoaster
pros: Pay benefits the friends u make the wfh option and if you end up with a ln amazing manager thatâs a plus.
cons: AHT just needs to be removed period. Be ready to deal with condescending comments from your lead when you donât make metrics. YOU WILL BE MADE TO FEEL ITS YOUR FAULT REGARDLESS of how much you plead your case. Youâre trying to explain yourself to people who either havenât taken calls in years or none at all EVER. If you start to excel in one or two areas be prepared to fail in another one. Time cards are unnecessarily made difficult to complete by far the worse Iâve experienced. Did you know you can fail a time card evaluation because their outdated systems make you do things that a system would auto configure for u, MANUALLY! Iâd give examples but donât even have the energy.

title: On the mend ...
pros: Barclays has had a revolving door of CEOs over the years until the most recent one who has provided the stability and clarity of purpose. There is now a plan a five year plan, which the firm is marching behind. People are warm, the culture is collegial, it is still 3 days hybrid, with decent benefits.
cons: But decades of muddling along and lack of investment in an operating model, processes, technology/systems/infrastructure within a complex organization with a risk averse culture to boot is not easy to fix. One wonders how much of this is due to the nature of banking that is highly regulated, cultural norms of the region. The focus is always on costs (so headcount cuts are always around the corner) with AI now being sprinkled as an elixir for productivity.

title: Supportive Culture with Room for Growth â A Long-Term Perspective
body: Supportive Culture with Room for Growth â A Long-Term Perspective
pros: Exceptional empathy and flexibility during personal challenges - Collaborative, inclusive culture that values employee well-being and work-life balance. - Strong loyalty rewards: Recognizes dedication and long-term contributions. - Opportunities for meaningful impact in a globally respected organization.
cons: Career progression can feel stagnant; promotions are infrequent and not always merit-based. - Limited clarity on pathways to align personal circumstances with career growth. - Transitions between regions may come with unspoken career penalties, despite business needs.

title: Emotional Distress? Here's your place
pros: Decent base salary. Nice renovated building? That's about it.
cons: Management style leaves a lot to be desired. Forever changes to management. New, unskilled managers come in and throw Federal mandates and practices out the window. You don't if you're coming or going. Everything changes 2 to 3 times a year. Constantly threatening your employment. Their way or the highway whether it's right or wrong. Favoritism is classic since beginning in Henderson. Bonuses are slim to none. Not based on work ethic and performance. Based on how well you get along with the manager and how you help the boss complete THEIR job tasks, not how well you do your job. No bonus scale for the employees to see with their own eyes. No explanation of how they decided on the amount of your bonus. Just "Well we gave ya this." Nice shiny new building. Same attitudes. Due to all the layoffs/terminations, the workload is unbearable and they work you like a dog expecting you to perform as well as Ai, robots! Or, there's the door. That threat is ALWAYS lingering.

title: Not a loyal employer, Fake âDEIâ Culture
body: Not a loyal employer, Fake âDEIâ Culture
pros: There are lots of perks to working at a big firm, but are they really worth your mental wellbeing? I was based at the 745 7 Ave corporate building. The people are generally friendly but itâs an intense work culture.
cons: Lots of bureaucracy. Management will ask for your input and do the exact opposite. Highly surveilled environment, no privacy. Layoffs are constant, so no job security. The bank is also profits from so much of the violence happening around the world, but pretends to be âDEI friendly.â Nonstop work culture, little work/life balance. Quick to exploit and quick to layoff. Not a loyal employer.

title: Promotions, benefits, & getting lost in the machine
body: Promotions, benefits, & getting lost in the machine
pros: I regularly get tickets to basketball games at Barclays, mens and womens, you just need to be on top of it. Nice holiday events, and events throughout the year on WHP campus. Good perks , discounts on hotels, gym memberships, and vacation rentals
cons: Cheap/frugal employer; 3rd time in less than a year getting a new manager; Internal promotions are really hard to come by, I heard its more luck than anything. To get a promotion you need to switch teams. Vacation time is standard and insurance is ok; definitely room for improvement

title: Micro Managed Job From Hell
pros: Barclays has a decent pay. Not bombarded with an overfill of calls depending on what department youâre in.
cons: Management and higher ups are all over the place. You are constantly being graded on new information that changes constantly. There is no foundation to new information given. There is a lot of favoritism that goes on within higher ups so unless youâre scratching someoneâs back constantly say good bye to any future promotions. Youâre treated very replaceable in any role of any department. There is a lack of support which is crucial in this role.

title: The best of times, the worst of times
body: The best of times, the worst of times
pros: Flexibility is decent with 2 days in office compared to 3+ for others, Whippany campus is modern. You can be exposed to a wide variety of technology. Good opportunities to migrate to other countries as well as Barclays has a global footprint.
cons: Sudden layoffs are common. Senior people laid off while cheaper employees remain. Pay is below expectation and bonuses are spartan. I made more going somewhere else and my new employer isnât even a bank. Whippany campus is nice but it is overcrowded. If youâre not in the right clique youâll get thrown under the bus and possibly selected for layoffs. Lots of scandals and just plain screw ups including a past CEO who had ties to Jeffrey Epstein.

cons: You're going to be no one if you're working as non revenue generating role, like you're a ghost here. Manager used me for their comfort such as they work from home while they tell their team members to work in office. Micromanaging is so peak at this place that you will be mentally stressed. I had to announce when I start work, go to restroom, get lunch, get off from work, literally every time I was away from keyboard. Conservative policy on trading floors such as wearing collared shirts, no phone usage, etc. On-boarding process is so mess that you will need to figure out everything and process of getting access to something will take forever because it has to go through so many different people. My manager gave me false information about HR policy so that I had to double check with HR.

title: External hires, enter at your own risk!
body: External hires, enter at your own risk!
pros: The salary/benefits are pretty good given that it's a mid-level job (5-7 years of experience). They offer some folks a remote work option which is nice to see as other banks have quit doing that.
cons: The HR process there is a disaster. After receiving an offer, I had to call the recruiter multiple times over a 4 week period asking where the offer documents were. Onboarding is another disaster. I spent the first two weeks without an email address...which is bizarre to me. I spent the first one month without Microsoft Teams. I then spent 3 months begging almost 10-15 diff IT admins for access to datasets and software. There were a few people on the team I directly worked with that gave off the attitude of "stop asking questions, just go do what I tell you to do" which is pretty demoralizing for new hires.

title: It's a Bank. You get what you expect
body: It's a Bank. You get what you expect
pros: Easy job. Great benefits. Okay environment. Not really as cutthroat as feared; though everyone seems to live in suspicion of management. Good place to ride out till retirement if all you want is to clock in 9 to 5 in a comfortable office building with fitness club.
cons: Boring. Old fashioned. Bureaucratic. Old tech staying alive with band-aids and staples, although this is common at the banks. Very much a British white boys club. US teams rarely get funding for extras like travel or training, while UK people of same level are constantly coming over for 3 week excursions.

title: Global World-Renowned Bank without the High Stress Burnout of GS, JP, etc
body: Global World-Renowned Bank without the High Stress Burnout of GS, JP, etc
pros: Barclays is becoming more salary competitive so definitely satisfied with the salary. Everyone is so nice and pleasant to work with. NJ office is state of the art, NYC is slowly being converted as well. Hybrid schedule, 2 days in office. Good benefits, lots of internal mobility. There's a reason many people have been here for 10+ years. Barclays is quickly becoming a bigger name in the space.
cons: -Still a bank, so many regulations to adhere to -Slow to get things done at times -Resourcing is hard to come by -Tech is on the older side but its quickly ramping up
